module-7-clean-student-records.label: Module Project: Clean Student Records
module-7-clean-student-records.summary: Grow one small cleaner from a raw file preview into a shareable cleaned output file.

sketch0.title: A messy student file lands in your inbox

mp_3.title: Skip bad rows and collect records

mp-1-read-student-file.title: Read the student file
mp-1-read-student-file.prompt: A student group emailed you `data/students.csv` and asked what is inside it. Read the file and print every raw row without extra blank lines so you can inspect the data before cleaning it.
mp-1-read-student-file.hint: Open the file, loop through each line, and print the stripped line.

help.concept: A real cleaning job usually starts by inspecting the raw file before changing anything.
help.hint_2: Inside the loop, print `line.strip()` to avoid extra blank lines.

mp-1-read-student-file.starterCode: # Read data/students.csv and print every line without extra blank lines.

mp-2-clean-one-row.title: Clean and validate one row
mp-2-clean-one-row.prompt: Start from the raw-file preview you already built. Keep that context, then add `clean_row(line)` so one row becomes a dictionary with a cleaned name and integer score. Read the first row again and print the cleaned dictionary.
mp-2-clean-one-row.hint: Leave the file shape familiar: define one helper, then reuse the file to test it on the first row.

help.concept: A cleaning function gives the rest of the program one dependable shape for each valid row.
help.hint_2: Return `{"name": ..., "score": ...}` with a title-cased name and an integer score.

mp-2-clean-one-row.starterCode:
with open("data/students.csv") as file:
    for line in file:
        print(line.strip())

# Keep the raw-file preview above as your starting point.
# Next, write clean_row(line) so one row becomes a cleaned dictionary.
def clean_row(line):
    # Split the row, clean the name, and convert the score to int.
    pass

with open("data/students.csv") as file:
    first_row = file.readline()

# Print the cleaned first row dictionary.
print(clean_row(first_row))

mp-3-skip-bad-rows.title: Skip bad rows and collect records
mp-3-skip-bad-rows.prompt: Keep `clean_row(line)` from the last step. Now build a list named `records` by looping through the file, appending valid cleaned rows, and skipping `ValueError` rows so the cleaner keeps moving.
mp-3-skip-bad-rows.hint: Reuse `clean_row(line)` inside the loop instead of re-cleaning the pieces by hand.

help.concept: In real data files, some rows are broken. A cleaner should keep good rows and quietly move past bad ones.
help.hint_2: Append inside `try`, then skip `ValueError` rows in `except`.

mp-4-write-clean-records.title: Write cleaned records
mp-4-write-clean-records.prompt: Start from the cleaned-record builder you already have. Keep that logic, then write the valid rows to `output/clean_students.csv` and replace the preview print with a short completion message.
mp-4-write-clean-records.hint: Turn the working `records` list into a deliverable file and a quick confirmation.

help.concept: The final step of a cleaning job is delivering a clean artifact, not just printing data to the screen.
help.hint_1: Create `output/` if needed, then open `output/clean_students.csv` in write mode.
help.hint_2: Write one cleaned record per line, then print how many clean records were saved.

try-module-7-clean-student-records-sketc.title: Clean the first row from the file
try-module-7-clean-student-records-sketc.prompt: Open `data/students.csv`, read the first row, clean the name and score, and print `Ava:92`.
try-module-7-clean-student-records-sketc.hint: Read the first line from the file, split it, clean both pieces, and print them with a colon.

help.concept: This card practices cleaning a real row from the same file shape used in the project.
help.hint_1: Use `with open("data/students.csv") as file:` and `first_row = file.readline()`.
help.hint_2: Split the row, use `.strip().title()` for the name, and print `name:score`.

try_module_7_clean_student_records_sketc.prompt: Open `data/students.csv`, read the first row, clean the name and score, and print `Ava:92`.

sketch-1.title: A messy student file lands in your inbox
sketch-1.bodyMarkdown:
A student group exported a file of names and scores, but some rows are messy and some are broken. They do not need a lecture about data cleaning; they need a small Python tool that reads the file, keeps the good records, skips the bad ones, and writes something usable.

This module project grows one careful step at a time. Each starter is the previous canonical solution plus the next realistic job.
